series.title: mm/mempolicy: NUMA mempolicy mismatch during remote access
series.author: Lin Ruifeng <linruifeng4@huawei.com>

patches.title: [RFC PATCH] mm/mempolicy: NUMA mempolicy mismatch during remote access

review.summary: This RFC reports a NUMA mempolicy mismatch in the SVA IOPF path: because page faults are handled asynchronously by kworkers, get_vma_policy() falls back to get_task_policy(current) using the kworker's mempolicy instead of the original process's task-level policy. There is no code diff to review.
review.review_inline:
Author: Lin Ruifeng <linruifeng4@huawei.com>

mm/mempolicy: NUMA mempolicy mismatch during remote access

This RFC has no code diff attached.  The description is a problem report
asking for suggestions.  I traced the relevant code paths below.

The diagnosis is correct.  The call chain for SVA IOPF is:

  iommu_sva_handle_iopf()        [kworker context]
    -> iommu_sva_handle_mm()
         -> handle_mm_fault(..., FAULT_FLAG_REMOTE, ...)
              -> do_anonymous_page()
                   -> alloc_anon_folio()
                        -> vma_alloc_folio()
                             -> vma_alloc_folio_noprof()
                                  -> get_vma_policy(vma, addr, order,
                                                    &ilx)

In get_vma_policy():

> struct mempolicy *get_vma_policy(struct vm_area_struct *vma, ...)
> {
>     pol = __get_vma_policy(vma, addr, ilx);  /* vma->vm_policy */
>     if (!pol)
>         pol = get_task_policy(current);      /* falls back to kworker */

When there is no VMA-level policy (mbind() was not called, only
set_mempolicy() was used by the user process), get_task_policy(current)
returns the kworker thread's mempolicy, not the original task's.
FAULT_FLAG_REMOTE is set at the call site in iommu_sva_handle_mm()
but get_vma_policy() has no visibility into it.

There is a second related issue: vma_alloc_folio_noprof() also passes
numa_node_id() as the preferred nid to folio_alloc_mpol_noprof(), which
is the NUMA node of the kworker, not the faulting process.

Some thoughts on possible fix directions:

  1. mm->owner (struct task_struct __rcu *) is available from the
     vm_area_struct.  A remote-fault-aware path could read
     rcu_dereference(vma->vm_mm->owner)->mempolicy in place of
     current->mempolicy.  mm->owner can be NULL (all threads of a
     group may have exited while the mm is still alive) so a NULL
     check and appropriate fallback is required.

  2. A more general approach: add a task_struct * or mempolicy *
     field to struct vm_fault, populated when FAULT_FLAG_REMOTE is
     set, and thread it through to get_vma_policy() and
     vma_alloc_folio_noprof() so the nid hint is also correct.

  3. Bind-time snapshot: capture the mempolicy into the iopf_group
     or SVA domain at iommu_sva_bind_device() time and use it for
     all subsequent fault handling in that domain.

The problem description is accurate.  It would help to include a
reproducer and a proposed fix in the next revision.

series.title: kernel/fork: validate exit_signal in kernel_clone()
series.author: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] kernel/fork: validate exit_signal in kernel_clone()

review.summary: The patch adds an exit_signal validity check inside kernel_clone() to reject clone() calls with exit_signal values outside [0, _NSIG] (e.g. 128 from clone_flags=0x80), covering all callers including sys_ia32_clone() that were previously unprotected.
review.review_inline:
On Mon, Mar 16 2026, Deepanshu Kartikey wrote:
> kernel/fork: validate exit_signal in kernel_clone()

The fix is correct in principle.  I checked the following:

- valid_signal() uses "sig <= _NSIG", so exit_signal=0 (no death
  signal) returns true and is correctly accepted by the new check.
- sys_fork() and sys_vfork() hardcode exit_signal=SIGCHLD (17), which
  is unaffected.
- kernel_thread() and user_mode_thread() derive exit_signal from
  "(flags & CSIGNAL)"; in practice callers pass 0 or SIGCHLD, both
  valid.
- clone3() already validates in copy_clone_args_from_user(), so the
  new check is a redundant but harmless double-check on that path.
- copy_process() sets p->exit_signal=-1 unconditionally for
  CLONE_THREAD, so the args->exit_signal value is ignored there anyway;
  rejecting out-of-range values for threaded clones is still correct
  tightening of the ABI.

One real issue to address:

> diff --git a/kernel/fork.c b/kernel/fork.c
> --- a/kernel/fork.c
> +++ b/kernel/fork.c

[ ... ]

>  /*
>   *  Ok, this is the main fork-routine.
>   *
>   * It copies the process, and if successful kick-starts
>   * it and waits for it to finish using the VM if required.
>   *
>   * args->exit_signal is expected to be checked for sanity by the caller.
>   */

This comment directly contradicts what the patch does.  Moving the
check into kernel_clone() is the whole point; the comment should be
updated (e.g. "args->exit_signal is validated inside kernel_clone()")
so future callers are not misled into skipping validation or adding
redundant checks of their own.

> +	if (!valid_signal(args->exit_signal))
> +		return -EINVAL;
>  	/*
>  	 * Determine whether and which event to report to ptracer.  When

Nit: a blank line between the new return statement and the opening /*
of the next block would match the style used throughout the rest of
kernel_clone().

series.title: mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK
series.author: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK

review.summary: The patch fixes a real race under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK where a concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER can split the VMA in the narrow window inside vma_start_read(), returning a stale VMA with a shrunk vm_end to mfill_atomic()'s loop, causing folio_add_new_anon_rmap() to WARN when dst_addr falls outside the bounds. The fix re-validates dst_addr on each iteration and re-acquires the VMA lock when a split is detected.
review.review_inline:
From: reviewer
Subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop
         under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK

I traced the race through vma_start_read() in mm/mmap_lock.c.
The first seqcount check (READ_ONCE(vma->vm_lock_seq) ==
READ_ONCE(mm->mm_lock_seq.sequence)) passes, vm_refcnt is
incremented, then __vma_start_write() runs concurrently: it sets
WRITE_ONCE(vma->vm_lock_seq, mm_lock_seq) and shrinks vma->vm_end
via __split_vma(). After mmap_write_unlock() increments mm_lock_seq,
the second seqcount check in vma_start_read() sees vm_lock_seq !=
mm_lock_seq (they diverged), so vma_start_read() returns success
with a stale, already-split VMA. The bug is real.

> +	       /*
> +		* Under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK, a concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER can

The opening /* is indented with a tab followed by seven spaces
instead of two tabs. The rest of the comment body uses two tabs
correctly. checkpatch.pl will flag this.

> +		if (state.dst_addr < state.vma->vm_start ||
> +		    state.dst_addr >= state.vma->vm_end) {
> +			mfill_put_vma(&state);
> +			state.dst_start = state.dst_addr;
> +			state.len = dst_start + len - state.dst_addr;
> +			err = mfill_get_vma(&state);
> +			if (err)
> +				break;
> +		}

I verified that mfill_get_vma() calls uffd_mfill_lock(ctx->mm,
state->dst_start, state->len), which calls validate_dst_vma(vma,
dst_start + len). Setting state.len to the remaining length means
validate_dst_vma checks that the full remaining range fits in the
new VMA. If the split-off VMA is smaller or is no longer
uffd-registered, mfill_get_vma() returns -ENOENT, the loop breaks,
and mfill_atomic() returns the bytes copied so far. That is the
correct behavior.

Note that the initial call to mfill_get_vma() before the loop
passes state.len == 0 (zero-initialized), so validate_dst_vma
trivially passes there. The re-lookup after a split is therefore
stricter than the original first call. That asymmetry is
pre-existing and not introduced by this patch.

The new block is not guarded by #ifdef CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK. Under
!CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK the loop holds mmap_read_lock throughout,
which excludes concurrent mmap_write_lock(), so the condition can
never trigger -- dead code, not a regression. Wrapping it in
#ifdef CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK would make the intent match the commit
message and the comment.

I also verified mfill_copy_folio_retry(): after the patch fires and
sets state.dst_start = state.dst_addr, any subsequent retry uses
the updated dst_start. uffd_lock_vma() is called with the updated
state.dst_start (still within the re-locked VMA), and
validate_dst_vma checks state.dst_start + state.len == the original
dst_start + len, so the retry re-lock is consistent.

Fix the comment indentation (tab + 7 spaces -> two tabs); optionally
add the #ifdef CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK guard. The functional fix is
correct.

series.title: mm/memcontrol: batch memcg charging in __memcg_slab_post_alloc_hook
series.author: Hui Zhu <hui.zhu@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH mm-unstable] mm/memcontrol: batch memcg charging in __memcg_slab_post_alloc_hook

review.summary: Batch memcg charging in __memcg_slab_post_alloc_hook by scanning ahead to find contiguous objects whose slabs share the same pgdat, then issuing one __obj_cgroup_charge/__consume_obj_stock call per run. The approach is correct but batch_bytes is declared as unsigned int instead of size_t, silently truncating obj_size * run_len on 64-bit when the product exceeds 2^32.
review.review_inline:
Hui Zhu <zhuhui@kylinos.cn>

mm/memcontrol: batch memcg charging in __memcg_slab_post_alloc_hook

The batching approach is sound.  I verified:

 - Error path: kmem_cache_free_bulk() uncharges only objects whose
   obj_ext->objcg was assigned, so objects in the current batch (not
   yet assigned when __obj_cgroup_charge() fails) are correctly left
   uncharged.  Objects skipped due to alloc_slab_obj_exts() failure
   have obj_exts == 0, so memcg_slab_free_hook() returns early and
   never touches their obj_exts -- same as before.

 - get/put_slab_obj_exts() are not refcounted (they call
   metadata_access_enable/disable()); calling them once per object in
   the inner assignment loop is fine.

 - skip_next advancement: i = i + run_len + 1 correctly skips the one
   object whose alloc_slab_obj_exts() failed in the lookahead loop.

 - Stock paths (__refill_obj_stock sets cached_objcg before
   __account_obj_stock is called) satisfy the "call after
   __refill_obj_stock" precondition in both the stock-hit and
   stock-miss branches.

One type issue:

> diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
> --- a/mm/memcontrol.c
> +++ b/mm/memcontrol.c

[ ... ]

> +		unsigned int batch_bytes;
> +		size_t run_len = 0;

> +		batch_bytes = obj_size * run_len;

batch_bytes is unsigned int, but obj_size is size_t and run_len is
size_t, so obj_size * run_len is evaluated as size_t and then truncated
to 32 bits.  On a 64-bit kernel any batch where obj_size * run_len >=
4 GB wraps silently, causing memcg to be undercharged for the whole
run.  This is the same truncation that already exists in the
single-object path where obj_size is passed to __consume_obj_stock()
(which also takes unsigned int), but multiplication makes it reachable
at much smaller per-object sizes.

The truncated value then propagates to:

> +		if (!stock || !__consume_obj_stock(objcg, stock, batch_bytes)) {
> +			if (__obj_cgroup_charge(objcg, flags, batch_bytes, &remainder))

__obj_cgroup_charge() takes size_t so batch_bytes is zero-extended back
to 64 bits, but the value is already wrong.

> +		__account_obj_stock(objcg, stock, batch_bytes,
> +				    pgdat, cache_vmstat_idx(s));

__account_obj_stock() takes int nr.  If the truncated unsigned int
value exceeds INT_MAX, the implicit conversion makes nr negative,
decrementing NR_SLAB_RECLAIMABLE_B or NR_SLAB_UNRECLAIMABLE_B rather
than incrementing it.

Should batch_bytes be declared as size_t instead of unsigned int?

series.title: Docs/mm: document the OOM killer
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document the OOM killer

review.summary: This patch fills in the Documentation/mm/oom.rst stub with an overview of OOM killer victim selection, the OOM reaper, process_mrelease, and sysctl knobs. One function name in the OOM reaper description is factually wrong.
review.review_inline:
Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document the OOM killer

Verified against mm/oom_kill.c:

- oom_badness() scoring: RSS + swap entries + pgtable pages, adjusted
  by oom_score_adj * totalpages / 1000.  Matches the description.
- oom_unkillable_task(): checks is_global_init() and PF_KTHREAD.  The
  claim "Kernel threads and init are never eligible" is correct.
- constrained_alloc(): totalpages baseline per constraint type is
  accurate.
- MMF_UNSTABLE is set at the top of __oom_reap_task_mm(), MMF_OOM_SKIP
  is set at the end of oom_reap_task() after all retries.  Ordering in
  the docs matches.
- sysctl table: panic_on_oom range [0,2], oom_dump_tasks defaults to 1,
  oom_kill_allocating_task defaults to 0.  All match.
- process_mrelease() calls __oom_reap_task_mm() directly.  Description
  is accurate.

One factual error in the OOM reaper section:

> +It walks the victim's VMAs in reverse and calls
> +``unmap_page_range()`` to release physical pages.

The reaper does not call unmap_page_range().  The actual call is
zap_vma_for_reaping().  The loop in __oom_reap_task_mm() is:

  mas_for_each_rev(&mas, vma, 0) {
          if (vma->vm_flags & (VM_HUGETLB|VM_PFNMAP))
                  continue;
          if (vma_is_anonymous(vma) || !(vma->vm_flags & VM_SHARED)) {
                  if (zap_vma_for_reaping(vma))
                          ret = false;
          }
  }

Also worth noting in the docs: hugetlb and pfnmap VMAs are skipped
entirely by the reaper.

Please replace ``unmap_page_range()`` with ``zap_vma_for_reaping()``
and mention that hugetlb/pfnmap VMAs are not reaped.

series.title: mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
series.author: <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v1] mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE

review.summary: The patch guards DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE behind CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE in the enum, sysfs name table, and vaddr.c switch, preventing users from setting those actions on THP=n kernels where the underlying madvise() calls would silently fail with -EINVAL.
review.review_inline:
From: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE

The motivation and approach look correct: on CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE=n
kernels, madvise.c guards MADV_HUGEPAGE/MADV_NOHUGEPAGE under #ifdef in
both its validation and its dispatch switch, so any damon_madvise() call
with those values would silently return -EINVAL.  Hiding the enum values
and the sysfs action names under the same guard is the right fix.

A couple of things to look at:

> diff --git a/include/linux/damon.h b/include/linux/damon.h
> --- a/include/linux/damon.h
> +++ b/include/linux/damon.h

[ ... ]

>  * @DAMOS_HUGEPAGE:	Call ``madvise()`` for the region with MADV_HUGEPAGE.
>  * @DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE:	Call ``madvise()`` for the region with MADV_NOHUGEPAGE.

The kernel-doc comment lines for @DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and @DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE are
not wrapped in #ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE, while the corresponding
enum values below are.  Documenting enum values that do not exist in a
given build is a minor inconsistency; consider guarding these two lines as
well.

> +#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
>  	DAMOS_HUGEPAGE,
>  	DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,
> +#endif
>  	DAMOS_LRU_PRIO,

Removing DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE from the enum shifts the
integer values of DAMOS_LRU_PRIO and all later actions by -2 on
CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE=n builds.  The sysfs interface is string-based
(action_show/action_store iterate damos_sysfs_action_names matching by
.action enum value, not by table index), so the kernel sysfs ABI is not
broken.

However, tools/testing/selftests/damon/sysfs.py contains a hardcoded
integer mapping used to validate the drgn-dumped kernel struct field:

    action_val = {
        'willneed': 0, 'cold': 1, 'pageout': 2,
        'hugepage': 3, 'nohugeapge': 4,
        'lru_prio': 5, 'lru_deprio': 6,
        ...
    }
    assert_true(dump['action'] == action_val[scheme.action], ...)

On a CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE=n kernel, DAMOS_LRU_PRIO is 3, not 5,
so every entry after 'pageout' is wrong.  The existing test case only uses
action='pageout' (value 2, unaffected by this patch), so it will not fail
today -- but any future test exercising lru_prio or higher on a THP=n
build will assert incorrectly.  Should this mapping be made conditional on
CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE, or replaced with a dynamic lookup?

> diff --git a/mm/damon/vaddr.c b/mm/damon/vaddr.c

[ ... ]

> +#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
>  	case DAMOS_HUGEPAGE:
>  		madv_action = MADV_HUGEPAGE;
>  		break;
>  	case DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE:
>  		madv_action = MADV_NOHUGEPAGE;
>  		break;
> +#endif

The existing default: branch returns 0 for unsupported actions, so on
THP=n builds removing these case labels is safe -- no missing case, no
fallthrough.  Verified that damos_sysfs_action_names is guarded the same
way, so scheme->action can never hold these values on THP=n.

Minor note on the commit message: MADV_HUGEPAGE and MADV_NOHUGEPAGE are
defined unconditionally in include/uapi/asm-generic/mman-common.h; what
is guarded by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is their dispatch in madvise.c.
The description would be slightly more precise if it said "their madvise()
handling is guarded" rather than "they are not available".

series.title: Docs/mm: document Boot Memory
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Boot Memory

review.summary: Fills in the bootmem.rst documentation stub with a description of the memblock allocator, its data structures, iteration macros, and the transition to the buddy allocator. Two factual inaccuracies need to be corrected before merging.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Boot Memory

Thanks for filling in this stub.  I cross-checked the described functions
and macros against mm/memblock.c and include/linux/memblock.h and found
two factual errors.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst b/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst
> --- a/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst

[ ... ]

> +- ``for_each_mem_range()`` iterates over free ranges (memory minus
> +  reserved).

This is not what the macro does.  Looking at include/linux/memblock.h:

  #define for_each_mem_range(i, p_start, p_end) \
    __for_each_mem_range(i, &memblock.memory, NULL, NUMA_NO_NODE, \
                         MEMBLOCK_HOTPLUG | MEMBLOCK_DRIVER_MANAGED, \
                         p_start, p_end, NULL)

The type_b argument is NULL, so no region type is subtracted.  The
macro iterates over all memory regions while skipping those flagged
HOTPLUG or DRIVER_MANAGED.  Reserved regions are not excluded.  The
"memory minus reserved" description belongs to a different iterator
(__for_each_mem_range with &memblock.reserved as type_b).  Could you
correct the description?

[ ... ]

> +The function ``free_area_init()`` is called by architecture code to set up
> +nodes and zones.

In the current tree, free_area_init() is a static function in mm/mm_init.c
invoked from mm_core_init_early(), which is itself called from
start_kernel() in init/main.c.  Architecture code does not call it
directly.  Could you update this to reflect the actual call chain?

review.summary: This series adds HMM_PFN_REQ_MIGRATE to enable combined fault+migration in a single page-table walk via hmm_range_fault(), eliminating the extra walks currently required. The approach is sound but the implementation has a compilation error in the !CONFIG_MIGRATION stub, a deadlock in the test driver, and a missing error check before migration proceeds.
review.review_inline:
From: <reviewer>
Subject: Re: [PATCH v6 0/6] Migrate on fault for device pages

Thanks for the series.  I reviewed the page-table walk locking,
folio refcount symmetry in the PMD migration path, and the test
driver plumbing.  A few issues below.

--- [PATCH v6 3/6] mm/hmm: do the plumbing for HMM to participate in migration ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/migrate.h b/include/linux/migrate.h
> --- a/include/linux/migrate.h
> +++ b/include/linux/migrate.h
> @@ -104,6 +104,15 @@ static inline void softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked(...)
>  	WARN_ON_ONCE(1);
>
>  	spin_unlock(ptl);
> +
> +enum migrate_vma_info {
> +	MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_NONE = 0,
> +	MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_COMPOUND = MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_NONE,
> +};
> +
> +static inline enum migrate_vma_info hmm_select_migrate(struct hmm_range *range)
> +{
> +	return MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_NONE;
>  }

This hunk places the enum declaration and the hmm_select_migrate() stub
inside the body of softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked(), between
spin_unlock(ptl) and the closing brace.  A function definition nested
inside another function is not valid C; this will fail to compile with
CONFIG_MIGRATION=n.  The enum and the stub need to appear after the
closing brace of softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked(), before the
#endif /* CONFIG_MIGRATION */ line.

--- [PATCH v6 5/6] mm: add new testcase for the migrate on fault case ---

> +static int do_fault_and_migrate(struct dmirror *dmirror,
> +				struct hmm_range *range)
> +{
> +	struct migrate_vma *migrate = range->migrate;
> +	int ret;
> +
> +	mmap_read_lock(dmirror->notifier.mm);
> +
> +	/* Fault-in pages for migration and update device page table */
> +	ret = dmirror_range_fault(dmirror, range);

dmirror_range_fault() calls mmap_read_lock(mm) internally before each
call to hmm_range_fault().  The outer mmap_read_lock() already taken
above means the same task tries to acquire mm->mmap_lock for read
twice.  If a writer is enqueued between the two acquisitions, the task
blocks on the inner down_read() while still holding the outer one,
deadlocking against the waiting writer.  Either remove the outer
mmap_read_lock() here and call hmm_range_fault() directly (after
setting range->notifier_seq), or keep the outer lock and call
hmm_range_fault() directly so the inner lock is not attempted.

> +	pr_debug("Migrating from sys mem to device mem\n");
> +	migrate_hmm_range_setup(range);
> +
> +	dmirror_migrate_alloc_and_copy(migrate, dmirror);
> +	migrate_vma_pages(migrate);
> +	dmirror_migrate_finalize_and_map(migrate, dmirror);
> +	migrate_vma_finalize(migrate);

The return value of dmirror_range_fault() is saved in ret but not
checked before calling the migration helpers.  If dmirror_range_fault()
returns an error, the hmm_pfns array is in an indeterminate state yet
migrate_hmm_range_setup() still reads it to populate migrate->src[].
The existing dmirror_migrate_to_device() checks the error from
migrate_vma_setup() before proceeding.  Should there be an early return
on ret != 0 here?

> +static int dmirror_fault_and_migrate_to_device(struct dmirror *dmirror,
> +					       struct hmm_dmirror_cmd *cmd)
> +{
> +	unsigned long src_pfns[PFNS_ARRAY_SIZE] = { 0 };
> +	unsigned long dst_pfns[PFNS_ARRAY_SIZE] = { 0 };
> +	struct migrate_vma migrate = { 0 };

[ ... ]

> +	migrate.src = src_pfns;
> +	migrate.dst = dst_pfns;
> +	migrate.flags = MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_SYSTEM;
> +
> +	range.migrate = &migrate;
> +	range.hmm_pfns = src_pfns;

migrate.start and migrate.end are left at zero from the
zero-initializer and are never set in this function or in the
do_fault_and_migrate() loop.  migrate_hmm_range_setup() then computes:

    migrate->npages = (migrate->end - migrate->start) >> PAGE_SHIFT;

which is 0/0 >> PAGE_SHIFT = 0, so the conversion loop never runs and
migrate->src[] is never populated.  migrate_vma_pages() and
migrate_vma_finalize() then operate on an empty set, making the
migration a silent no-op.  Do patches 3 or 4 arrange for
hmm_range_fault() to write back range->start/end into
migrate->start/end?  If not, migrate_hmm_range_setup() should use
range->start and range->end instead of migrate->start/end for the
npages calculation, or the caller must set them per iteration.

> diff --git a/lib/test_hmm_uapi.h b/lib/test_hmm_uapi.h
> -#define HMM_DMIRROR_MIGRATE_TO_SYS   _IOWR('H', 0x03, ...)
> -#define HMM_DMIRROR_SNAPSHOT         _IOWR('H', 0x04, ...)
> -#define HMM_DMIRROR_EXCLUSIVE        _IOWR('H', 0x05, ...)
> +#define HMM_DMIRROR_MIGRATE_ON_FAULT_TO_DEV _IOWR('H', 0x03, ...)
> +#define HMM_DMIRROR_MIGRATE_TO_SYS           _IOWR('H', 0x04, ...)
> +#define HMM_DMIRROR_SNAPSHOT                 _IOWR('H', 0x05, ...)

This renumbers every ioctl from 0x03 onward.  The selftests are updated
in the same patch so they stay in sync, but existing compiled test
binaries will silently invoke the wrong operation.  Could the new ioctl
be appended at the end of the existing list to avoid the renumbering?

series.title: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC]  mm/mempolicy: introduce socket-aware weighted interleave
series.author: Rakie Kim <rakie.kim@sk.com>

patches.title: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC] [RFC PATCH 0/4] mm/mempolicy: introduce socket-aware weighted interleave
[RFC PATCH 1/4] mm/numa: introduce nearest_nodes_nodemask()
[RFC PATCH 2/4] mm/memory-tiers: introduce socket-aware topology management for NUMA nodes
[RFC PATCH 3/4] mm/memory-tiers: register CXL nodes to socket-aware packages via initiator
[RFC PATCH 4/4] mm/mempolicy: enhance weighted interleave with socket-aware locality

review.summary: This series introduces socket-aware weighted interleave by restricting candidate nodes to the current CPU's physical package before applying weights, backed by a new memory_package topology layer and CXL initiator-based registration. Several correctness issues need addressing before it is ready.
review.review_inline:
From: Rakie Kim <rakie.kim@sk.com>
Subject: [RFC PATCH 0/4] mm/mempolicy: introduce socket-aware
         weighted interleave

Hi Rakie,

Thanks for posting this.  The idea is sound and the benchmark numbers
are compelling.  A few issues to work through before this is ready.

--- Patch 1/4 ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/numa.h b/include/linux/numa.h
> +static inline int nearest_nodes_nodemask(int node,
> +					 const nodemask_t *mask,
> +					 nodemask_t *out)
> +{
> +	return 0;
> +}

The real implementation begins with nodes_clear(*out), but this
non-NUMA stub leaves *out uninitialised on a "successful" 0 return.
Any future caller that checks return == 0 and then iterates *out
will read garbage.  Please add nodes_clear(*out) here.

--- Patch 2/4 ---

The stubs added under the !CONFIG_NUMA section of memory-tiers.h:

> +static inline int mp_get_package_nodes(int nid, nodemask_t *out)
> +{
> +	return 0;
> +}

Same pattern: returns 0-success without initialising *out.
mp_get_package_cpu_nodes and mp_get_package_memory_only_nodes have
the same problem.  For !CONFIG_NUMA these are dead through
mempolicy.c, but the stubs should still be safe.  Please add
nodes_clear(*out) to each.

--- Patch 3/4 ---

> +static int cxl_region_find_nearest_node(struct cxl_region *cxlr)
> +{
> +	struct cxl_region_params *p = &cxlr->params;
> +	int i, numa_node;
> +
> +	for (i = 0; i < p->nr_targets; i++) {
> +		cxled = p->targets[i];
> +		cxlmd = cxled_to_memdev(cxled);
> +		numa_node = dev_to_node(&cxlmd->dev);
> +		if (numa_node != NUMA_NO_NODE)
> +			return numa_node;
> +	}
> +	return NUMA_NO_NODE;
> +}

This picks the NUMA node of the first endpoint with a valid nid as
the initiator for the entire region.  For a CXL region interleaved
across endpoints attached to different CPU sockets, this single
initiator nid determines which package the DAX node is registered
with.  If targets[0] happens to land on socket 1 while the majority
of interleave targets serve socket 0, the region ends up in the
wrong package.  Is a single-initiator heuristic sufficient here?
If so, could you document the selection criterion and explain why it
is correct for cross-socket interleaved regions?

--- Patch 4/4 ---

> +static int policy_resolve_package_nodes(struct mempolicy *policy,
> +					nodemask_t *mask)
> +{
> +	unsigned int node, ret = 0;

ret is unsigned but receives the return value of mp_get_package_nodes()
which returns int.  The function itself is declared to return int, so
please make ret int to match.

> +			if (nodes_empty(*mask))
> +				goto out;

This goto exits with ret == 0 and *mask empty -- a silent success
with no usable nodes.  The callers only test the return value:

> +	if (policy_resolve_package_nodes(policy, &mask))
> +		mask = policy->nodes;

A zero return with an empty mask is treated as success.  In
weighted_interleave_nodes() the subsequent next_node_in(node,
empty_mask) then returns MAX_NUMNODES, which propagates back out
through mempolicy_slab_node() as an invalid node.  The empty-mask
path should either return a non-zero error or be documented as
structurally unreachable (i.e., if mp_get_package_nodes always
includes the queried node in its output mask, explain why the second
intersection can never be empty).

The same zero-return-empty-mask risk exists in
read_once_policy_package_nodemask(): if policy_resolve_package_nodes
returns 0 with an empty package_mask, nodes_weight() returns 0 and
weighted_interleave_nid() silently falls back to numa_node_id(),
ignoring the user's configured policy nodes entirely.

> +static unsigned int read_once_policy_package_nodemask(
> +				struct mempolicy *pol, nodemask_t *mask)
> +{
> +	nodemask_t package_mask;
> +
> +	barrier();
> +	if (policy_resolve_package_nodes(pol, &package_mask))
> +		memcpy(mask, &pol->nodes, sizeof(nodemask_t));
> +	else
> +		memcpy(mask, &package_mask, sizeof(nodemask_t));
> +	barrier();

The barriers are meant to stabilise pol->nodes for safe iteration,
matching the approach in read_once_policy_nodemask().  But
policy_resolve_package_nodes() reads pol->nodes again independently
inside its body, without any barrier relative to the outer pair.
Under a concurrent cpuset rebind the two reads of pol->nodes could
observe different values, making package_mask inconsistent with the
snapshot used for the weight loop.  Would it make sense to snapshot
pol->nodes once (as read_once_policy_nodemask does) and pass that
snapshot into policy_resolve_package_nodes() rather than letting it
re-read pol->nodes?

series.title: userfaultfd: unassigned vma leads to a potential unreleased locks
series.author: Edward Adam Davis <eadavis@qq.com>

patches.title: [PATCH next] userfaultfd: unassigned vma leads to a potential unreleased locks

review.summary: mfill_get_vma() failed to release mmap_lock and map_changing_lock on validation failure because state->vma was only assigned at the out: label, causing mfill_put_vma() to bail early when state->vma is NULL. The fix moves the assignment to immediately after uffd_mfill_lock() succeeds so all error paths release locks correctly.
review.review_inline:
Edward Adam Davis <eadavis@qq.com>

userfaultfd: unassigned vma leads to a potential unreleased locks

Verified the lock release paths in mfill_get_vma() and mfill_put_vma().

> diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> @@ -218,6 +218,7 @@ static int mfill_get_vma(struct mfill_state *state)
>  	if (IS_ERR(dst_vma))
>  		return PTR_ERR(dst_vma);
>  
> +	state->vma = dst_vma;

Moving this here is correct.  mfill_put_vma() guards on state->vma == NULL
and returns immediately without calling up_read() or uffd_mfill_unlock().
Before this patch, any failure path that jumped to out_unlock before
reaching the original out: label left both mmap_lock and map_changing_lock
unreleased.

> @@ -257,7 +258,6 @@ static int mfill_get_vma(struct mfill_state *state)
>  		goto out_unlock;
>  
>  out:
> -	state->vma = dst_vma;
>  	return 0;
>  
>  out_unlock:
>  	mfill_put_vma(state);

Verified that every goto out_unlock in mfill_get_vma() is only reachable
after down_read(&ctx->map_changing_lock) at line 226, so the up_read()
inside mfill_put_vma() is always balanced.  The release order in
mfill_put_vma() -- up_read(map_changing_lock) first, then mmap_lock via
uffd_mfill_unlock() -- is correct.

mfill_state is zero-initialized in mfill_atomic(), so state->vma starts
as NULL and there is no double-assignment issue.

Reviewed-by: <reviewer>

series.title: zram: propagate read_from_bdev_async() errors
series.author: Sergey Senozhatsky <senozhatsky@chromium.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] zram: propagate read_from_bdev_async() errors

review.summary: Changes read_from_bdev_async() from void to int so allocation failures (-ENOMEM) propagate up through read_from_bdev() to the existing error-handling chain in zram_bio_read(), fixing a silent data-corruption bug where failed async reads were previously reported as success.
review.review_inline:
Sergey Senozhatsky <senozhatsky@chromium.org>

zram: propagate read_from_bdev_async() errors

Verified the patch against the call chain:
  read_from_bdev_async() -> read_from_bdev() -> zram_read_page()
  -> zram_bvec_read() -> zram_bio_read()

> -static void read_from_bdev_async(struct zram *zram, struct page *page,
> -				 u32 index, unsigned long blk_idx,
> -				 struct bio *parent)
> +static int read_from_bdev_async(struct zram *zram, struct page *page,
> +				u32 index, unsigned long blk_idx,
> +				struct bio *parent)

Both early failure returns (kmalloc_obj and bio_alloc) occur before
bio_inc_remaining(parent), so the parent bio's remaining count is
not affected on the error path.  No accounting imbalance.

> -	read_from_bdev_async(zram, page, index, blk_idx, parent);
> -	return 0;
> +	return read_from_bdev_async(zram, page, index, blk_idx, parent);

zram_bio_read() already checks zram_bvec_read() < 0 and sets
bio->bi_status = BLK_STS_IOERR, which is the right place to handle
this.  In the mixed case (some bvecs submitted async before the
failing one), the parent bio completes only after all outstanding
child bios call bio_endio(parent), so bi_remaining stays balanced.

Previously the silent return-0 on allocation failure let the parent
bio complete successfully with unread page data.  The fix is correct.

series.title: mm/damon/core: fix wrong damon_set_regions() argument
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm/damon/stat: fix wrong argument to damon_set_regions()
[PATCH] mm/damon/core: fix wrong damon_set_regions() argument

review.summary: This two-patch series fixes a copy-paste bug where addr_unit (a scale factor, not a count) was wrongly passed as the nr_ranges argument to damon_set_regions() in both core.c and stat.c; in both call sites only a single damon_addr_range is passed, so the correct value is 1.
review.review_inline:
SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon: fix wrong damon_set_regions() nr_ranges argument

Verified the damon_set_regions() signature:

  int damon_set_regions(struct damon_target *t,
          struct damon_addr_range *ranges,
          unsigned int nr_ranges, unsigned long min_region_sz)

The third parameter is nr_ranges -- the length of the ranges array.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
> --- a/mm/damon/core.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/core.c

[ ... ]

> -	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, addr_unit, min_region_sz);
> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);

In damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default(), &addr_range is a
pointer to a single on-stack struct damon_addr_range, so nr_ranges must
be 1.  Passing addr_unit is wrong: damon_set_regions() iterates
ranges[0]..ranges[nr_ranges-1], so when addr_unit != 1 (e.g. PAGE_SIZE,
which lru_sort.c allows via module_param) the loop walks far past the
single valid element.  The fix is correct.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/stat.c b/mm/damon/stat.c
> --- a/mm/damon/stat.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/stat.c

[ ... ]

> -	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, addr_unit, min_region_sz);
> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);

Same bug, same fix.  No locking, refcount, or error-path concerns in
either change -- both are single-line constant argument corrections.

series.title: mm/damon: let DAMON be paused and resumed
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [RFC PATCH 00/10] mm/damon: let DAMON be paused and resumed
[RFC PATCH 01/10] mm/damon/core: introduce damon_ctx->paused
[RFC PATCH 02/10] mm/damon/sysfs: add pause file under context dir
[RFC PATCH 03/10] Docs/mm/damon/design: update for context pause/resume feature
[RFC PATCH 04/10] Docs/admin-guide/mm/damon/usage: update for pause file
[RFC PATCH 05/10] Docs/ABI/damon: update for pause sysfs file
[RFC PATCH 06/10] mm/damon/tests/core-kunit: test pause commitment
[RFC PATCH 07/10] selftests/damon/_damon_sysfs: support pause file staging
[RFC PATCH 08/10] selftests/damon/drgn_dump_damon_status: dump pause
[RFC PATCH 09/10] selftests/damon/sysfs.py: check pause on assert_ctx_committed()
[RFC PATCH 10/10] selftets/damon/sysfs.py: pause DAMON before dumping status

review.summary: This series introduces a damon_ctx->pause flag that lets kdamond idle (sleeping one sample_interval per loop iteration, processing only damon_call() callbacks) while preserving all internal state, and exposes it via a new 'pause' sysfs file under each context directory.
review.review_inline:
From: <reviewer>
To: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 01/10] mm/damon/core: introduce damon_ctx->paused

Thanks for the series.  The core mechanism is clean and the threading
model is sound: ctx->pause is read and written exclusively from the
kdamond thread (writes happen inside damon_commit_ctx(), invoked from
kdamond_call() callbacks), so there is no data race.  Two items below
are worth addressing before posting non-RFC.

> diff --git a/include/linux/damon.h b/include/linux/damon.h
> --- a/include/linux/damon.h
> +++ b/include/linux/damon.h
> @@ -811,6 +811,8 @@ struct damon_ctx {
>  	 * intervals tuning
>  	 */
>  	unsigned long next_intervals_tune_sis;
> +	/* pause kdamond main loop */
> +	bool pause;
>  	/* for waiting until the execution of the kdamond_fn is started */
>  	struct completion kdamond_started;

The new field lands in the '/* private: internal use only */' section,
but 'pause' is a user-settable API parameter in the same sense as
'ops', 'addr_unit', 'min_region_sz', 'adaptive_targets', and 'schemes',
all of which live in the '/* public: */' section.  Should 'pause' be
moved to the public section and added to the struct kdoc comment
alongside the other @-tagged public fields?

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
> --- a/mm/damon/core.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/core.c
> @@ -2978,6 +2979,13 @@ static int kdamond_fn(void *data)
>  		 * kdamond_merge_regions() if possible, to reduce overhead
>  		 */
>  		kdamond_call(ctx, false);
> +		while (ctx->pause) {
> +			if (kdamond_need_stop(ctx))
> +				goto done;
> +			kdamond_usleep(ctx->attrs.sample_interval);
> +			/* allow caller unset pause via damon_call() */
> +			kdamond_call(ctx, false);
> +		}
>  		if (!list_empty(&ctx->schemes))
>  			kdamond_apply_schemes(ctx);
> +		else
> +			damos_walk_cancel(ctx);

The pause loop calls kdamond_call() but never damos_walk_cancel().
Consider the following sequence: a kernel thread calls damos_walk(),
which installs ctx->walk_control and then blocks in
wait_for_completion().  Concurrently, a second thread queues a
damon_call() callback that calls damon_commit_ctx() with pause=true.
kdamond processes that callback in the kdamond_call() call just before
the pause check, sets ctx->pause = true, and then enters the pause
loop with ctx->walk_control still non-NULL.  Since neither
kdamond_apply_schemes() nor the else-branch damos_walk_cancel() is ever
reached, the damos_walk() caller sits in wait_for_completion()
indefinitely -- until the context is unpaused or the kdamond is
stopped (which eventually reaches damos_walk_cancel() at the 'done:'
label).

Should the pause loop call damos_walk_cancel() once before entering
the first kdamond_usleep(), so a pending walk is released promptly
rather than for the entire pause duration?

Everything else checks out.  The kdamond_usleep(sample_interval) in
the pause loop with a potentially-zero interval is consistent with the
existing usleep call in the main loop.  The stop path via
kdamond_need_stop() -> goto done -> damos_walk_cancel() is correct.
The sysfs ctx->pause = sys_ctx->pause assignment in
damon_sysfs_apply_inputs() runs inside a damon_call() callback, so
it is safe.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Page Reclaim
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Page Reclaim

review.summary: This patch fills in the page_reclaim.rst documentation stub with a high-level overview of the page reclaim subsystem, covering kswapd/direct reclaim triggers, priority-based scanning, LRU lists (classic and multi-gen), shrinkers, memcg reclaim, and NUMA demotion.
review.review_inline:
On Mon, Mar 16 2026, Kit Dallege wrote:

[PATCH] Docs/mm: document Page Reclaim

Fill in the page_reclaim.rst stub created in commit 481cc97349d6
("mm,doc: Add new documentation structure") as part of
the structured memory management documentation following
Mel Gorman's book outline.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst b/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst
> --- a/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst

[ ... ]

> +Reclaim Priority
> +================
> +
> +The reclaim path operates at decreasing priority levels (from
> +``DEF_PRIORITY`` down to 0).  At each level, a larger fraction of the LRU
> +lists is scanned.  At the default priority, only 1/4096th of pages are
> +considered; at priority 0, the entire list is scanned.

Verified: DEF_PRIORITY=12 in include/linux/mmzone.h; scan fraction
is computed as (total >> sc->priority), giving 1/4096 at priority
12 and the full list at priority 0.  Correct.

[ ... ]

> +Classic LRU
> +-----------
> +
> +The classic scheme uses four LRU lists per lruvec: active and inactive for
> +both anonymous and file-backed pages.  This approximates a second-chance
> +(clock) algorithm:
> +
> +- Pages start on the inactive list when first allocated.
> +- If accessed again while on the inactive list, they are promoted to the
> +  active list.

Verified against mm/swap.c: folio_add_lru() does not set the active
flag for classic LRU (only the MGLRU in-fault path does so), so new
folios land on the inactive list.  folio_mark_accessed() promotes an
inactive folio to active.  Correct.

> +LRU Batching
> +------------
> +
> +To avoid taking the lruvec lock on every page access, LRU operations are
> +batched per-CPU (``mm/swap.c``).  Functions like ``folio_add_lru()`` and
> +``folio_mark_accessed()`` queue pages into per-CPU folio batches that are
> +drained to the actual LRU lists periodically or when the batch is full.

Verified: cpu_fbatches in mm/swap.c holds per-CPU folio_batch
fields (lru_add, lru_activate, etc.); folio_add_lru() calls
folio_batch_add_and_move() which drains when the batch is full.
Correct.

[ ... ]

> +Memory Cgroup Reclaim
> +---------------------
> +
> +When memory cgroup limits are exceeded, reclaim targets only the pages
> +belonging to that cgroup.  Each memory cgroup has its own lruvec per node,
> +so the scanner can isolate its pages without disturbing the rest of the
> +system.  ``try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages()`` is the entry point for
> +cgroup-scoped reclaim.

Verified: try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages() exists in mm/vmscan.c and
calls do_try_to_free_pages() with sc.target_mem_cgroup set.  Correct.

Also verified: shrinker callbacks count_objects()/scan_objects() are
declared in include/linux/shrinker.h and the implementation lives in
mm/shrinker.c.  Cross-references to Documentation/mm/oom.rst,
Documentation/mm/multigen_lru.rst, and
Documentation/mm/unevictable-lru.rst all resolve to existing files.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Shared Memory Filesystem
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Shared Memory Filesystem

review.summary: Fills in the shmem.rst documentation stub with a description of tmpfs/shmem internals covering page cache integration, swap, huge pages, accounting, and fallocate. Two factual inaccuracies were found: the shadow-entry description misplaces where shadows land for shmem evictions, and the fallocate section omits the FALLOC_FL_KEEP_SIZE flag.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Shared Memory Filesystem

Good start filling in the stub.  Two factual issues worth fixing:

> +Page Cache Integration
> +======================
> +
> +tmpfs uses the kernel's page cache (xarray) to index its pages by file
> +offset.  When a page is read or faulted in, the page cache is checked
> +first.  If the page has been swapped out, a swap entry is found in its
> +place, and the page is swapped back in transparently.
> +
> +When a page is added to the cache for a tmpfs file, it replaces any
> +existing swap entry at that offset.  When a page is evicted by reclaim,
> +a swap entry takes its place.  Shadow entries (see
> +Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst) may also be stored to support working
> +set detection.

The shadow-entry claim is misleading for shmem.  For regular file
pages, workingset_eviction() stores a shadow in the xarray slot via
__filemap_remove_folio().  For shmem, however, the eviction path in
vmscan.c goes through the folio_test_swapcache() branch and passes the
shadow to __swap_cache_del_folio(), which stores it in the swap table
(shadow_to_swp_tb()), not in the shmem inode's xarray.  The xarray
slot for a swapped-out shmem page holds only the swap entry, never a
shadow entry.  Could the paragraph clarify that working-set shadows
for shmem are stored in the swap cache rather than in the page-cache
xarray?

> +fallocate
> +=========
> +
> +tmpfs supports ``fallocate()`` to preallocate space for a file.
> +Preallocated pages are allocated and inserted into the page cache
> +immediately, guaranteeing that subsequent writes will not fail with
> +``ENOSPC``.
> +
> +``FALLOC_FL_PUNCH_HOLE`` is also supported: it removes pages from a range
> +of the file and returns them to the filesystem's free pool.  This is used
> +by applications that want to release portions of a tmpfs file without
> +truncating it.

shmem_fallocate() accepts two flags:

  if (mode & ~(FALLOC_FL_KEEP_SIZE | FALLOC_FL_PUNCH_HOLE))
          return -EOPNOTSUPP;

FALLOC_FL_KEEP_SIZE is missing from the description.  When set, space
is reserved beyond the current i_size without extending it.  The
section says fallocate "guarantees subsequent writes will not fail with
ENOSPC", but that guarantee only holds for the pre-reserved range; it
would be clearer to mention FALLOC_FL_KEEP_SIZE and note that it
preallocates without changing the visible file size.

series.title: mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources

review.summary: This patch fixes damon_stat to monitor all System RAM regions rather than just the biggest one, by walking all System RAM resources and computing a single bounding range from the first region's start to the last region's end. The fix is correct for the stated goal.
review.review_inline:
SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources

The approach of walking all System RAM regions and using a single
bounding range [first.start, last.end] is correct.  I verified the
following:

- walk_system_ram_res() traverses resources in ascending address order
  (via walk_res_desc/find_next_res with start = res.end + 1 after each
  step), so damon_stat_system_ram_walk_fn() correctly captures the
  lowest start and the highest end across all System RAM regions.

- The arg.walked boolean correctly detects the no-System-RAM case and
  the return value of walk_system_ram_res() does not need to be checked
  separately.

- damon_add_target() is called before damon_stat_set_monitoring_region(),
  and damon_destroy_ctx() calls damon_destroy_targets() internally, so
  the error path through free_out: is leak-free.

- addr_range.end = damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(arg.res.end + 1, addr_unit)
  produces a correct exclusive end, consistent with damon_addr_range
  semantics.

- damon_set_regions() is called with nr_ranges=1, which is correct.

One question:

> +static unsigned long damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(resource_size_t ra,
> +		unsigned long addr_unit)
> +{
> +	/*
> +	 * Use div_u64() for avoiding linking errors related with __udivdi3,
> +	 * __aeabi_uldivmod, or similar problems.  This should also improve the
> +	 * performance optimization (read div_u64() comment for the detail).
> +	 */
> +	if (sizeof(ra) == 8 && sizeof(addr_unit) == 4)
> +		return div_u64(ra, addr_unit);
> +	return ra / addr_unit;
> +}

damon_res_to_core_addr() in mm/damon/core.c is byte-for-byte identical
to this new helper.  It is currently static, so stat.c cannot call it
directly.  Would it make sense to make damon_res_to_core_addr() non-static
and declare it in include/linux/damon.h, rather than duplicating the
logic here?

Aside from that code-duplication question the logic looks correct to me.

series.title: mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
series.author: Leno Hou via B4 Relay <devnull+lenohou.gmail.com@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v3 0/2] mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
[PATCH v3 1/2] mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
[PATCH v3 2/2] mm/mglru: maintain workingset refault context across state transitions

review.summary: Two-patch series introducing a draining state for MGLRU enable/disable transitions to prevent cgroup OOM kills by ensuring folios remain visible to at least one reclaim mechanism, plus shadow entry context tracking to preserve workingset refault semantics across state changes.
review.review_inline:
From: Reviewer

mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
mm/mglru: maintain workingset refault context across state transitions

The problem statement is real and the general approach (intermediate
draining state + per-folio gen check instead of global flag) is
sound. A few issues need addressing before this is ready.

> diff --git a/include/linux/mm_inline.h b/include/linux/mm_inline.h
> +static inline bool lru_gen_draining(void)
> +{
> +	DECLARE_STATIC_KEY_FALSE(lru_drain_core);
> +
> +	return static_branch_unlikely(&lru_drain_core);
> +}

DECLARE_STATIC_KEY_FALSE is an extern declaration; the corresponding
DEFINE_STATIC_KEY_FALSE(lru_drain_core) must live in a .c file.  The
vmscan.c diff is truncated so I cannot confirm the definition is
there.  Similarly, lru_gen_change_state() must be modified to call
static_branch_enable/disable for lru_drain_core at the beginning and
end of the transition -- those changes are also not visible.  If
either is missing the feature never activates and the fix is a no-op.
Can you confirm both are present in the full patch?

> diff --git a/mm/swap.c b/mm/swap.c
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled()) {
> +	if (folio_lru_gen(folio) != -1) {
>  		lru_gen_inc_refs(folio);
>  		return;
>  	}

This change in folio_mark_accessed() is correct.  When enabling MGLRU,
lru_gen_enabled() becomes true before fill_evictable() completes, so
some folios are still on traditional LRU (gen == -1).  The old code
would have called lru_gen_inc_refs() on such folios incorrectly; the
new code falls through to the traditional path.  The symmetric case
during disable (folio still on MGLRU lists after lru_gen_enabled()
becomes false) is also handled correctly.

> -	bool active = folio_test_active(folio) || lru_gen_enabled();
> +	bool active = folio_test_active(folio) || (folio_lru_gen(folio) != -1);

Same reasoning applies to lru_deactivate_file(): checking per-folio
gen state is more precise than the global flag during transitions.
Looks correct.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
> +	/* whether in lru gen scan context */
> +	unsigned int lru_gen:1;

[ ... ]

> +	sc->lru_gen = 0;
>  	nr_reclaimed = shrink_folio_list(&folio_list, pgdat, sc, &stat, false,

This sets lru_gen on the shared scan_control that was allocated by the
top-level reclaim entry point and threaded down.  The traditional and
MGLRU eviction paths are mutually exclusive within one shrink_lruvec
call, so for current code this is safe.  If the draining mechanism
(not visible in the truncated diff) calls both shrink_inactive_list
and evict_folios sequentially on the same sc, the ordering of these
assignments (0 then 1) must be carefully verified to ensure the flag
is set correctly before each shrink_folio_list call.

> +		.lru_gen = lru_gen_enabled(),
>  	};

This appears in both reclaim_clean_pages_from_list() and
reclaim_folio_list().  These functions receive a list of folios that
may have come from either MGLRU or traditional LRU lists regardless
of the current global state -- particularly during transitions.  Using
lru_gen_enabled() as a proxy tags all evictions from these paths as
MGLRU (or not) based solely on the current global flag, which will be
wrong for folios from the other system.  A per-folio check
(folio_lru_gen(folio) != -1) at the point of eviction would be more
correct, though I acknowledge these are not the primary paths
responsible for the reported OOM.

> diff --git a/mm/workingset.c b/mm/workingset.c
> -void *workingset_eviction(struct folio *folio,
>                            struct mem_cgroup *target_memcg);
> +void *workingset_eviction(struct folio *folio,
>                            struct mem_cgroup *target_memcg, bool lru_gen);

The workingset.c diff for patch 2 is not shown.  The current code
in workingset_refault() dispatches based on lru_gen_enabled():

    if (lru_gen_enabled()) {
        lru_gen_refault(folio, shadow);
        return;
    }

and workingset_test_recent() does the same.  The whole point of patch
2 is to instead dispatch based on the is_mglru bit encoded in the
shadow entry.  If workingset_refault() and workingset_test_recent()
still use lru_gen_enabled() after the patch, an MGLRU shadow entry
that refaults after MGLRU is disabled will be unpacked by the
traditional path against the nonresident_age counter, producing a
garbage refault distance and incorrect activation decisions.  Please
share the full workingset.c diff so this critical path can be
verified.

Also, lru_gen_eviction() and the traditional eviction path produce
different-format shadow entries (gen token with LRU_REFS_WIDTH bits vs
nonresident_age counter).  Adding the is_mglru bit on top of the
existing pack_shadow() encoding needs to fit within the EVICTION_SHIFT
bit budget.  The existing BUILD_BUG_ON in lru_gen_eviction() checks
LRU_GEN_WIDTH + LRU_REFS_WIDTH against the remaining bits; adding
WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT tightens that bound and likely needs a
corresponding update to that assertion.

Summary of items to address:
1. Confirm DEFINE_STATIC_KEY_FALSE(lru_drain_core) and the
   lru_gen_change_state() enable/disable calls are in the full diff.
2. Show the full workingset.c diff, particularly whether
   workingset_refault() and workingset_test_recent() dispatch on the
   is_mglru shadow bit rather than lru_gen_enabled().
3. Consider per-folio gen check in reclaim_clean_pages_from_list()
   and reclaim_folio_list() instead of lru_gen_enabled().
4. Verify the shadow entry bit layout with the added is_mglru bit and
   update the BUILD_BUG_ON accordingly.

series.title: selftests/mm: add folio_split() and filemap_get_entry() race test
series.author: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] selftests/mm: add folio_split() and filemap_get_entry() race test

review.summary: Adds a selftest for the race condition between folio_split() and filemap_get_entry(), where shmem hole-punching via MADV_REMOVE triggers folio_split() concurrently with lockless xarray reads in 16 reader threads.
review.review_inline:
Author: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>

selftests/mm: add folio_split() and filemap_get_entry() race test

The test structure looks correct: parent punches holes to drive
folio_split(), child runs 16 reader threads exercising
filemap_get_entry() locklessly.  The page-pattern fill/verify approach
cleanly distinguishes corruption from zeroing.  One logic issue below.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/folio_split_race_test.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/folio_split_race_test.c
> new file mode 100644

[ ... ]

> +static void *reader_thread(void *arg)
> +{
> +	struct reader_arg *ra = (struct reader_arg *)arg;
> +	[...]
> +		for (page_idx = (size_t)tid; page_idx < TOTAL_PAGES;
> +		     page_idx += NUM_READER_THREADS) {
> +			if (page_idx % PUNCH_INTERVAL >= 0 &&
> +			    page_idx % PUNCH_INTERVAL < PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR)
> +				continue;

`page_idx` is `size_t` (unsigned).  PUNCH_INTERVAL expands to the
integer literal 50, which is converted to size_t in the % expression,
yielding an unsigned result.  That means `page_idx % PUNCH_INTERVAL
>= 0` is always true -- it is a tautological comparison.  Clang with
-Wall warns about this (-Wtautological-unsigned-zero-compare).  The
intended guard is just:

    if (page_idx % PUNCH_INTERVAL < PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR)
        continue;

The run-time behaviour is unaffected (the always-true half does not
change which pages are skipped), but the dead sub-expression should be
dropped.

series.title: rust_binder: use AssertSync for BINDER_VM_OPS
series.author: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] rust_binder: use AssertSync for BINDER_VM_OPS

review.summary: The patch wraps BINDER_VM_OPS in AssertSync<vm_operations_struct> to satisfy Rust's Sync requirement for immutable statics, which broke after 'userfaultfd: introduce vm_uffd_ops' added a raw pointer field to vm_operations_struct. No functional change is introduced.
review.review_inline:
Author: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>

rust_binder: use AssertSync for BINDER_VM_OPS

Verified the following:

> -static BINDER_VM_OPS: bindings::vm_operations_struct = pin_init::zeroed();
> +static BINDER_VM_OPS: AssertSync<bindings::vm_operations_struct> = AssertSync(pin_init::zeroed());

AssertSync is declared #[repr(transparent)], so &BINDER_VM_OPS.0 is the
same address as &BINDER_VM_OPS. The identity check in check_vma() and
the assignment in register_with_vma() are therefore consistent and
correct.

> -    if !ptr::eq(vm_ops, &BINDER_VM_OPS) {
> +    if !ptr::eq(vm_ops, &BINDER_VM_OPS.0) {

Both sides of the comparison refer to the same address via the
transparent wrapper, so the VMA identity check is unchanged.

The broader SAFETY comment on `unsafe impl<T> Sync for AssertSync<T>`
is accurate: the same pattern was already in use for file_operations,
and wrapping zeroed C structs in a global is safe regardless of whether
the struct gains new pointer fields.

Looks good to me.

series.title: elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping
series.author: WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

patches.title: [PATCH 3/4] elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping

review.summary: Patch 3/4 bumps the ET_DYN alignment to at least PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order() so that the PIE load_bias is contpte-boundary aligned on arm64. The approach unconditionally applies this alignment to all ET_DYN binaries regardless of their segment alignment, which both reduces ASLR entropy for binaries that cannot benefit and forces a probe-mmap on every static-PIE exec on arm64/4K pages.
review.review_inline:
Usama Arif <uarif@meta.com>

elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping

> +			if (exec_folio_order())
> +				alignment = max(alignment,
> +					(unsigned long)PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order());

This sits right after the maximum_alignment() call in the first-PT_LOAD
ET_DYN block, so it feeds into both the PIE path (load_bias masking)
and the static-PIE path (the alignment > ELF_MIN_ALIGN branch). On
arm64 with 4K pages exec_folio_order() returns 4, so alignment is
always at least 64K after this change regardless of what the binary
requested.

WANG Rui's concern is well-founded. I see two concrete problems with
the unconditional bump:

1. Static-PIE probe-mmap regression.

   For ET_DYN without PT_INTERP the two paths are:

     if (alignment > ELF_MIN_ALIGN) {
         load_bias = elf_load(...);    /* probe mmap at addr 0 */
         vm_munmap(load_bias, total_size);
         load_bias &= ~(alignment - 1);
         elf_flags |= MAP_FIXED_NOREPLACE;
     } else
         load_bias = 0;               /* fast, no extra calls */

   Before this patch, a binary with p_align=4K produces
   maximum_alignment()=ELF_MIN_ALIGN=PAGE_SIZE, so the fast path is
   taken. After this patch alignment is always 64K > ELF_MIN_ALIGN,
   so every static-PIE exec on arm64/4K goes through an extra
   elf_load() probe and vm_munmap() even when the segments are only
   4K-aligned and contpte cannot fire. That is two extra syscalls on
   every exec for static-PIE binaries (ld.so invoked directly,
   static-pie applications) on arm64 with 4K pages.

2. ASLR entropy reduced for binaries that cannot benefit from contpte.

   For PIE (ET_DYN with PT_INTERP) the masking is:

     load_bias = ELF_ET_DYN_BASE + arch_mmap_rnd();
     if (alignment)
         load_bias &= ~(alignment - 1);

   A 64K-aligned load_bias only produces a 64K-aligned VMA if the
   segment vaddr is itself 0 mod 64K. A binary with p_align=4K can
   have a vaddr of e.g. 0x1000, so the mapped VMA starts at
   load_bias+0x1000 which is not 64K-aligned even after masking.
   Contpte does not fire for that mapping, yet exec_folio_order()
   bits of ASLR entropy are consumed on every exec of that binary.

Both problems would go away if the alignment bump were conditional on
the segments already being exec-folio-order aligned (vaddr, file
offset, and memsz all 64K-aligned), as WANG Rui suggests. Binaries
linked with p_align>=64K get the benefit; everything else keeps full
ASLR entropy and the static-PIE fast path.

One way to do this inside maximum_alignment() would be to only apply
the exec_folio_order floor when at least one PT_LOAD has
p_align >= (PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order()), so the ELF_MIN_ALIGN
guard in the static-PIE path still saves the fast path for
small-alignment binaries.

Could you clarify the intended scope? Is the goal to benefit binaries
that are already built with 64K-aligned segments (in which case the
constraint is needed), or to incentivise toolchains to adopt 64K
alignment (in which case the probe-mmap overhead is a deliberate
trade-off worth calling out in the commit message)?
